chunks.text: Nelson Morgan is Professor in Residence Emeritus in the EECS Department at UC Berkeley. He is the former Director of the International Computer Science Institute (ICSI) and led its Speech Group from 1988 to 2013. He received his Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering from UC Berkeley in 1980 as an NSF Fellow. Morgan has been working on signal processing and pattern recognition since 1974, with a primary emphasis on speech processing.

Morgan is a former Editor-in-chief of Speech Communication and has served on the IEEE Speech Technical Committee, IEEE Neural Networks Committee, and ISCA Advisory Council. He is a Fellow of both IEEE and the International Speech Communication Association (ISCA). In 1997, he received the Signal Processing Magazine best paper award. In 2022, he and collaborator Herve Bourlard received the IEEE James L. Flanagan Speech and Audio Processing Award for contributions to neural networks for statistical speech recognition.

Morgan developed the hybrid system approach to speech recognition, which uses neural networks probabilistically with Hidden Markov Models (HMMs). He was Principal Investigator for the DARPA EARS Novel Approaches project from 2002-2005, a U.S. government program focusing on long-term progress in speech recognition. He has over 200 publications including three books, with his most recent being a text on speech and audio signal processing co-authored with Ben Gold and Dan Ellis.

Morgan holds several patents in speech processing methods, including one used in millions of CDMA cell phones. His research interests include redesigning signal processing for speech recognition systems from first principles and using neural networks for designing new features. He is also working on a project to reduce the political influence of large campaign contributions through structural and technological means. He has served on various boards including ICSI, Sensory Inc., and the Toyota Technological Institute at Chicago.

Selected publications include works on deep vs. wide architectures for robust speech recognition (2013), biologically-inspired feature extraction (2012), and multiple layers in automatic speech recognition (2012). His book 'Speech and Audio Signal Processing: Processing and Perception of Speech and Music' was published in its second edition in 2011. Other publications cover parallelizing speech recognition (2010), research directions in speech recognition (2009), and RASTA processing of speech (1994).

Honors and awards include the 2022 SPS James L. Flanagan Speech and Audio Processing Award, being named an International Speech Communication Association (ISCA) Fellow in 2010, and becoming an Institute of Electrical & Electronics Engineers (IEEE) Fellow in 1999. His education includes a Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering from UC Berkeley in 1980. Research areas include Signal Processing (SP), and he is affiliated with the Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research Lab (BAIR) and International Computer Science Institute (ICSI).
